dialogues.text: You look like you need this more than I do, and I don't need it at all. Take the eighty. No strings, no story attached — I'm not in the story business.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. Nobody needs to know where it came from. Least of all you. Put it away and walk easy.
dialogues.no_response: Your call. Pride's an expensive habit, though. I've watched it bury better men than both of us.

dialogues.text: I don't ask questions, and I don't go looking for answers. Saves everybody the trouble. Eighty. Take it before I change my mood about it.
dialogues.yes_response: Smart. Walk away clean. Clean's the only thing in this neighborhood worth holding onto.
dialogues.no_response: Different people make different choices with their pride. I've made my peace with respecting it.

dialogues.text: I've seen worse situations than the one you're wearing on your face. Not many, I'll grant you. But a few. Take the eighty.
dialogues.yes_response: Right answer. Now go handle whatever it is you came down here to handle. Don't let me keep you.
dialogues.no_response: You sure about that? Take a look at yourself. That's not a face that should be turning down money.

dialogues.text: I've got eighty here that's been looking for a new home all week. Restless money. Could be yours, if you'll have it off my hands.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. Clean transaction, nothing owed in either direction. That's how I like to leave a thing.
dialogues.no_response: Your loss, friend. Money like this doesn't sit lonely for long. Somebody else'll give it a home.

dialogues.text: I don't do charity — charity's got conditions, and I've got none. But I do know how to recognize a man on a clock when one walks past me. Eighty.
dialogues.yes_response: Yeah. I can tell. Take it. You'll want it before this day's finished with you, trust me on that.
dialogues.no_response: Suit yourself. I've got other corners to be standing on. Good luck with the clock.

dialogues.text: This is clean money. Whatever you've decided about me from across the street — this part of it is clean. Eighty, and I'll swear to that much.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. What you do with it after is your business, and your business stays yours.
dialogues.no_response: That's a lot of principle for a man who looks the way you look right now. I'll allow it, though.

dialogues.text: I'm not going to insult you by pretending I don't see what's going on with you. It's written plain. Eighty. Take it and let's not name the thing.
dialogues.yes_response: Thought so. Go on, then. The day's not getting any longer while you stand here being polite.
dialogues.no_response: Okay. But the offer keeps. You change your mind, you know which corner. No judgment in it.

dialogues.text: You need it. I've got it. Everything past those two facts is just noise people make to feel complicated. Eighty.
dialogues.yes_response: Now walk. Don't look back at me — looking back's how people remember faces they shouldn't.
dialogues.no_response: They always come back around eventually. I'll be right here when you do. I'm not hard to find.

dialogues.text: I don't care what you're carrying. Genuinely, not my business. What I care about is that you're still upright and carrying it. Eighty toward that.
dialogues.yes_response: Go handle it, whatever it is. That's the whole point of money — handling things. Go.
dialogues.no_response: Alright. Good luck out there. And I mean that one straight, no angle on it.

dialogues.text: Last time I'm putting it on offer. Eighty. We don't have to be anything to each other for it — not friends, not even names. Just take it.
dialogues.yes_response: Good. We're not anything, and that's clean. Now go be something to whoever's waiting on you.
dialogues.no_response: Alright. Take care of yourself out there. The fog doesn't check a man's pride before it comes in.
